metadata.title: Write the Recipe: The Conductor's Score
metadata.playlist: Build the Recipe — Make Your Tool Conductor-Ready
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 5B brief — Nik's Recipe-Based Framework

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 4 of 6. The recipe is the conductor's score. It is a plain-language document that a conductor who has never seen your project can pick up and run — and more importantly, stop at the right moment. Not pseudocode. Not a flowchart. Plain English, one step per layer, a typed phase gate at every handoff, and typed TODO items for anything still unresolved. Before you write a single step, read the recipe standardization rules from the madison/recipes folder on GitHub — at build time, verify the current README there and reconcile the template with what you see. Recipes that do not follow the standardization rules are not machine-readable, and the assignment grades them as such. This episode builds the recipe top to bottom. Let's go.

props.segment: Write the Recipe: The Conductor's Score
props.command: Write the recipe: executive summary + 4 layer steps + typed phase gates + typed TODOs + conductor note.
props.runningText: read the madison/recipes standardization rules before writing — non-standard = not machine-readable…
output: Executive summary: 3–5 sentences what/who/how often/what success looks like
One step per layer with a typed phase gate after each
Five TODO types for anything unresolved

beats.narration_text: The recipe opens with an executive summary — three to five sentences that answer four questions in order. What does the pipeline do? A conductor reading this for the first time should understand the purpose of the run before they see any technical detail. Who runs it? The role, not a person's name. How often does it run? Daily, weekly, on-demand, triggered by an event. And what does a successful run produce? Not a system description — a concrete output. 'A daily ranked summary of relevant news items, verified and formatted for the primary user's review' is an executive summary. 'This pipeline collects data from three sources and processes it using AI' is not — it describes mechanics without answering the conductor's most important question, which is: what am I trying to produce here? Write the executive summary last, after you have written all the steps. It is easier to summarize something you have already specified than to specify something you have only summarized.

artifactLines: 1. What does the pipeline do? — purpose, not mechanics.
2. Who runs it? — role, not a name.
3. How often? — daily / weekly / on-demand / event-triggered.
4. What does a successful run produce? — a concrete output, not a system description.
Write last: easier to summarize what you've specified than to specify what you've summarized.
props.sparkLine: What. Who. How often. What success produces. Four questions.

beats.act: one step per layer with typed phase gates
beats.narration_text: The recipe body has one step per layer. Step one covers the external-to-raw ingest: what sources are pulled, what the raw output looks like, where it lands. Step two covers the raw-to-verified GIGO check: which quality gate rules apply, what happens to records that fail, where passing records land. Step three covers the verified-to-AI processing: what the AI does with the verified records, what fields it produces, what the output schema looks like. Step four covers the AI-to-report output: what the final artifact is, where it goes, who receives it. After each step, there is a phase gate section. The phase gate has three required sub-items: good_looks_like in plain language — what the conductor would see if the step succeeded; bad_looks_like in plain language — what signals failure; and judgment_type — one of PA, PF, TO, IJ, or EI — followed by a one-sentence rationale. The response field says what the conductor does: hard stop and notify, flag for review, log and continue. A step with no phase gate after it is a handoff with no contract. The conductor does not know when to stop.

props.artifactHeading: One step per layer, one gate per step
artifactLines: Step 1: external → raw/ — what sources, what raw output, where it lands.
Step 2: raw/ → verified/ — GIGO gate rules, what fails, where passing records land.
Step 3: verified/ → AI — what AI does, what fields it produces, output schema.
Step 4: AI → reports/ — final artifact, where it goes, who receives it.
Each step: phase gate with good_looks_like · bad_looks_like · judgment_type + rationale · response.
No gate after a step = no contract. Conductor doesn't know when to stop.
props.sparkLine: One step per layer. One gate per step. Judgment type required.

beats.narration_text: The recipe will have unresolved items. Every unresolved item gets a typed TODO tag. There are five types. DATA SOURCE: the pipeline needs a data source that has not been connected yet — what source, what it is expected to provide. DEFINE: a decision has not been made — a quality threshold, a field name, a routing rule. The TODO names the decision and who needs to make it. DEV: something requires implementation work — a node, a script, an integration. APPROVE: something needs a human approval before it can proceed — a data-use agreement, a security review, an account provisioning request. REPORT FIELD: a specific field in the output report is not yet defined — what it should contain, what format it should be in. Every open TODO needs a real blocker: 'waiting on DEV implementation' or 'pending external API access approval' are real blockers. 'I have not decided yet' is not a real blocker — it is a deferred decision. For those, name who makes the decision and by when. A recipe with untyped TODOs or open TODOs with no blocker signals that the recipe is a first draft that was never hardened.

props.artifactHeading: Every unresolved item gets a typed tag
artifactLines: DATA SOURCE: pipeline needs a source not yet connected — name it + what it provides.
DEFINE: a decision not yet made — name the decision + who makes it.
DEV: requires implementation work — name the component + what it must do.
APPROVE: needs human approval before proceeding — name the approval + who grants it.
REPORT FIELD: output field not yet defined — name it + expected format.
Open TODO needs a real blocker. 'Haven't decided' → name who decides + by when.
props.sparkLine: Type every TODO. Give every open one a real blocker.

beats.narration_text: The recipe closes with a conductor note — a paragraph, not a bullet list — on what the human must watch for that the recipe cannot encode. Domain knowledge. Edge cases the author encountered that surprised them. Timing constraints that are not obvious from the step descriptions. Service-level behaviors that are undocumented elsewhere. The conductor note is the difference between a recipe that is technically complete and one that is practically useful. A first-time conductor running from the recipe alone should be able to make better judgment calls because of the note. An example conductor note: 'The news RSS feed is unusually active on Monday mornings — a pull between 8am and 10am Eastern often returns three times the normal record count. This is expected behavior, not a source error. The IJ gate at Step 1 should account for this when checking the expected record range.' That single observation prevents a conductor from triggering a false PF on a healthy run. Write it from your own experience running the pipeline. If you have not run it enough times to write a useful note, that is the signal to run it more before submitting.

artifactLines: A paragraph — not a bullet list — of domain knowledge the recipe cannot capture.
Domain knowledge: timing quirks, undocumented service behavior, edge cases you encountered.
A first-time conductor should make better judgment calls because of this note.
Example: 'RSS feed triples in volume Monday 8–10am Eastern — expected, not an error. IJ gate should account for this.'
If you haven't run the pipeline enough to write this: run it more before submitting.
props.sparkLine: What surprised you running it. Write that paragraph.

beats.narration_text: One final rule that runs through every line of the recipe: it is plain language, not pseudocode. A recipe written in pseudocode or technical shorthand is only readable by someone with the same technical background as the author. A recipe written in plain language is readable by Claude Code, a colleague who has never used n8n, a project manager reviewing the architecture, and you at six months' distance when you have forgotten the technical details. The plain language rule does not mean avoiding technical terms — it means defining them when you use them. 'Run the Cleanup node in n8n' is plain language with a technical term. 'Execute workflow node delta on verified schema' is pseudocode. The test for any recipe sentence is: read it out loud. If a non-technical colleague would look confused, rewrite it. If they would ask 'and what does that mean,' add the definition. The conductor who follows your recipe may be the most capable AI agent available — but only if the recipe is unambiguous enough for them to apply it.

props.artifactHeading: Not pseudocode — readable by conductor, colleague, future you
artifactLines: Plain language: readable by Claude Code, a non-technical colleague, or you in 6 months.
Technical terms allowed — define them when you use them.
Test: read every sentence aloud. Confused look → rewrite. 'What does that mean?' → add definition.
Passes: 'Run the Cleanup node in n8n — merges, dedupes, and standardizes date format.'
Fails: 'Execute workflow node delta on verified schema.' — pseudocode, not plain language.
props.sparkLine: Read it aloud. If it needs explanation, it needs a rewrite.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Write the Recipe. Before writing, read the madison/recipes standardization rules — reconcile the template at build time. The recipe has six sections in order: executive summary of three to five sentences covering what, who, how often, and what success produces; one step per layer; a typed phase gate after each step with good_looks_like, bad_looks_like, judgment_type, and response; five typed TODO tags for unresolved items — DATA SOURCE, DEFINE, DEV, APPROVE, REPORT FIELD — with real blockers, not deferred decisions; and a conductor note paragraph of domain knowledge the recipe cannot encode. Plain language throughout — not pseudocode. A gate with no judgment type costs ten points. An open TODO with no real blocker signals a draft that was not hardened. Episode 5 is the /snickerdoodle audit.

props.artifactTitle: Write the Recipe: The Conductor's Score
artifactLines: Read madison/recipes standardization rules first — reconcile template at build time.
Executive summary: what · who · how often · what success produces (3–5 sentences).
One step per layer. One gate per step: good · bad · judgment_type · response.
TODOs: 5 types (DATA SOURCE · DEFINE · DEV · APPROVE · REPORT FIELD). Real blockers only.
Conductor note: domain knowledge paragraph. Plain language throughout — not pseudocode.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. This prompt turns your brand_config and brief into a first-draft recipe. Paste it into Claude — fill in every bracket before running. The prompt reads: I have a brand_config.json for a Madison tool. The config has these phase gates: [list each gate's step name, good_looks_like, bad_looks_like, and judgment_type]. The tool has these data sources: [list each source name and local_path]. The AI processing step does this: [one sentence from P3 or the config]. The primary user is: [role and situation from the config]. Write the first draft of the recipe in plain language. Include: an executive summary of three to five sentences covering what the pipeline does, who runs it, how often, and what a successful run produces. Then one step per layer — external to raw, raw to verified, verified to AI, AI to reports. After each step, write a phase gate section with three sub-items: good_looks_like, bad_looks_like, and judgment_type with a one-sentence rationale. For any step where a decision or implementation is still pending, add a typed TODO with the correct type and a real blocker. End with a conductor note paragraph of one observation about this specific pipeline that a first-time conductor would not know from reading the steps alone. Take the draft, read every sentence aloud, and rewrite any that sound like pseudocode.

props.segment: Write the Recipe: The Conductor's Score
props.command: Config phase gates: [list step + good + bad + judgment_type]. Data sources: [list]. AI step: [one sentence]. Primary user: [role + situation]. Write recipe draft: exec summary (what/who/how-often/success) · 4 layer steps · typed gate per step · typed TODOs with real blockers · conductor note paragraph.
props.runningText: read every sentence aloud after drafting — rewrite anything that sounds like pseudocode…

beats.narration_text: Write the Recipe: The Conductor's Score. Liam, in for Bear.
